Note that there are \({n \choose 4}\) ways to choose the four doors, then the contestant will randomly pick one of the three doors that does not have the least valuable prize, so they will have 3 choices. Therefore, the size of the sample space is \(3{n \choose 4}.\)
Now we consider the number of ways that \(Z\) can equal \(z.\) For this to happen, first the box with \(z\) dollars must be chosen. Then, at least one of the \(z-1\) boxes with less than \(z\) dollars must be chosen. Counting the number of ways that none of the remaining boxes are lower-valued to count the complement, we have that there are \({n-1 \choose 3} - {n-z \choose 3}\) ways to pick boxes such that the door with \(z\) dollars is not the lowest. This is because if all three remaining do not hold less than \(z\) dollars, then there are \(n-z\) boxes with more than \(z\) dollars to choose from and we must pick 3 boxes.
After the prizes are chosen so that there is a door with less than \(z\) dollars, the number of ways that the contestant can pick the exact door with \(z\) dollars is 1. Therefore, by naive definition of probability, we have that the PMF is \[
P(Z=z) = \frac{{n-1 \choose 3}- {n-z \choose 3}}{3{n \choose 4}}, \qquad z \in \{2, \dots, n\}.
\]




(Repeated Openings, Ch. 3) Monty Hall is hosting a version of his show with \(n \geq 3\) doors. Exactly one of the doors will hold a car. The contestant will initially choose a door, and then Monty will open \(k\) doors with goats that are not the contestant’s initial choice.
Monty has been forgetful recently, so he may repeatedly reveal the same door multiple times. On each of the \(k\) reveals, Monty will open one of the goat doors that the contestant did not initially choose with equal probabilities, where the doors he chooses to reveal are conditionally independent of the other reveals given which door holds the car. (Assume that this happens because e.g., the doors automatically close on their own and then Monty forgets what door he just opened.) This means that \(k\) can be higher than \(n.\) In fact, assume that \(k \geq n.\)

Explain in words why after \(k\) reveals, there must exist at least one door which was revealed more than once.
What is the distribution of the number of doors that Monty must open until he reveals a door he already opened before?
Find the probability that the car is behind the contestant’s initial door given that Monty kept opening unique doors until he repeated for the \(r\)th door he opened, with \(r \leq n.\) Find what your answer is equal to when \(r = n\), and give an intuitive explanation.







CautionSolution






This is the Pigeonhole Property. If there are only \(n-1\) doors that Monty can possibly reveal and Monty reveals at least \(n\) of them, then it is impossible that none was opened more than once as if this was true, he could have revealed doors at most \(n-1\) times.
This is similar to the birthday problem. Let \(X\) be the number of doors until Monty repeats a door. First note that the support of \(X\) is \(\{2, \dots, n\},\) since Monty must open at least 2 doors until he opens a repeat and if the contestant’s initial door holds a car, Monty can open the other \(n-1\) doors one time each and finally repeat on the \(n\)th try.
One thing that is useful to condition on is whether the contestant initially chose the door with the car or not. If they did choose the only car door, then Monty has \(n-1\) choices to choose from. Otherwise, he has \(n-2\) choices to choose from. So let \(C\) be the event that the contestant’s initial door holds the car. now we can write \[
P(X=x) = P(X=x|C)P(C) + P(X=x|C^c)P(C^c).
\] We know that \(P(C) = 1/n\) since there are \(n\) doors that the contestant can choose and only one of them holds the car. Now consider \(P(X=x|C)\) for some \(x \in \{2, \dots, n\}.\) Given \(C,\) Monty has \(n-1\) choices to choose from. Therefore, he must not repeat for the first \(x-1\) choices then finally repeat with one of the \(x-1\) doors he already opened on the \(x\)th opening. Therefore, \[
P(X=x|C) = \frac{x-1}{n-1}\prod_{i=1}^{x-1} \frac{n-i}{n-1}
\] Now consider \(P(X=x|C^c).\) This is equal to zero when \(x = n\) as Monty only has \(n-2\) choices to choose from. When \(x\) is less than \(n,\) we get that \[
P(X=x|C^c) = \frac{x-1}{n-2}\prod_{i=1}^{x-1}\frac{n-i-1}{n-2}.
\] Note that when \(x = n,\) the product becomes zero as \(n-(n-1)-1 = 0.\) Therefore, the above probability still holds when \(x = n.\) Therefore, we can write \[
P(X=x) = f(x) = \frac{x-1}{n(n-1)}\prod_{i=1}^{x-1}\frac{n-i}{n-1} + \frac{(x-1)(n-1)}{n(n-2)}\prod_{i=1}^{x-1}\frac{n-i-1}{n-2}.
\]
We want to find \(P(C|X=u).\) By Bayes’ Rule, \[
P(C|X=u) = \frac{P(X=u|C)P(C)}{P(X=u)}.
\] Plugging in our answer from part (a), we get that \[
P(C|X=u) = \frac{\frac{x-1}{n-1}\prod_{i=1}^{x-1}\frac{n-i}{n-1}}{f(u)}.
\] When \(u = n,\) we get that \(P(X=u) = f(u)\) is equal to the numerator due to the zero case we talked about before. Thus, we get a probability of 1. This makes sense as if Monty finally repeated on the \(n\)th opening, he must have opened the \(n-1\) doors that the contestant did not initially choose one time each, in which case the initial choice is the only door that can still hold the car.




(Limited Cars) Monty Hall has \(n\) cars, and he is planning to give them all away on his shows! Every day, Monty Hall will have one contestant playing his game, and each contestant will switch with probability \(p.\) The decision of each contestant to switch or not is independent. Suppose that \(n\) and \(p\) are known constants with \(n > 0.\)

What is the distribution of the number of games that Monty will have to host before all \(n\) cars have been given away?
Suppose \(m \leq n,\) with \(m\) an integer. Find the distribution of the number of cars given away after \(m\) games.
Put a part about explaining minimum in the next part.
Let \(N\) bethe number of games until all cars have been given away. Find the distribution of the number of cars given away after \(\min(N, n+1)\) games.







CautionSolution






First notice that the probability a contestant will win a car is \(\frac{2}{3}p+\frac{1}{3}(1-p) = \frac{1}{3}(1+p).\) Let \(N\) be the number of games until all cars have been given away. By the story of the Negative Binomial, we have that \(N-n \sim \textnormal{NBin}(n, \frac{1}{3}(1+p))\) since \(N-n\) counts the number of times the contestants fail to win the car. Therefore, using the Negative Binomial PMF, we have that \[
P(N = k) = P(N-n = k-n) = {k-1 \choose n-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}(1+p)\right)^n\left(\frac{1}{3}(2-p)\right)^{k-n},
\] with support \(k \in \{n, n+1, \dots,\}.\)
Let \(C_m\) be the number of cars given away after \(m\) games. By the story of the Binomial, we have that \(C_m \sim \text{Bin}(m, \frac{1}{3}(1+p)).\)
First consider the support. We can give away 0 cars after \(n+1\) games, and we can also give away all \(n\) cars. Therefore, the support is \(\{0, 1, \dots, n\}.\) Now, we make the important observation that we might as well assume that we play \(n+1\) games, where if all the games end up with a win, then the last car is taken back. The distribution of the number of cars given away in this scenario is equivalent to the one given in the problem as we give away cars until we reach \(n+1\) games or can’t give away cars anymore.
Now, the number of wins in the \(n+1\) games is Binomial as we discussed in part (b). The only difference is that we have to merge the PMF evaluation at \(n\) and \(n+1\) into one as they both imply \(n\) cars were given away. Therefore, the PMF is given by \[
P(C_{n+1} = k) = {n+1 \choose k}\left(\frac{1}{3}(1+p)\right)^k\left(\frac{1}{3}(2-p)\right)^{n-k+1}, k \in \{0, \dots, n-1\}
\] and \[
P(C_{n+1}=n) = \left(\frac{1}{3}(1+p)\right)^n\left(\frac{n+1}{3}(2-p) + \frac{1}{3}(1+p)\right).
\]
crumbs: 3  Random Variables and their Distributions

title: 5  Afterword
text: Through this book, we have proven the Universality of the Monty Hall.

title: Appendix A — Distributions
text:
A.1.1 Bernoulli
Suppose \(X \sim \textnormal{Bern}(p).\) Then, \(X\) has PMF \[
P(X = 1) = p, \; P(X = 0) = 1-p.
\] In other words, \(X\) is 1 with \(p\) probability and 0 otherwise.


A.1.2 Binomial
Suppose \(X \sim \text{Bin}(n, p).\) Then, \(X\) has PMF \[
P(X = k) = {n \choose k} p^k(1-p)^{n-k},
\] with support \(k \in \{0, \dots, n\}.\)
If \(I_1, \dots, I_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}\textnormal{Bern}(p),\) then \(I_1 + \dots + I_n \sim \text{Bin}(n, p).\)
If we have \(n\) independent trials each with probability \(p\) of succeeding, then the number of successes over the \(n\) trials is distributed as \(\text{Bin}(n, p).\)
Through the story, \(\text{Bin}(1, p)\) is the same distribution as \(\textnormal{Bern}(p)\) since either the single trial succeeds or it doesn’t.


A.1.3 Geometric
Suppose \(X \sim \textnormal{Geom}(p).\) Then, \(X\) has PMF \[
P(X = k) = (1-p)^kp
\] with support \(k \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots \}.\)
If we have trials with independent success probability \(p\) and we kept doing trials until we get a success, the number of failures is distributed as \(\textnormal{Geom}(p).\)


A.1.4 First Success
Suppose \(X \sim \textnormal{FS}(p).\) Then, \(X\) has PMF \[
P(X = k) = (1-p)^{k-1}p
\] with support \(k \in \{1, 2, \dots \}.\)
If we have trials with independent success probability \(p\) and we kept doing trials until we get a success, the number of trials, inclduing the success, is distributed as \(\textnormal{FS}(p).\)
If \(X \sim \textnormal{FS}(p),\) then \(X-1 \sim \textnormal{Geom}(p)\) as \(X-1\) is the number of trials when we do not include the success. Similarly, if \(X \sim \textnormal{Geom}(p),\) then \(X+1 \sim \textnormal{FS}(p).\)


A.1.5 Negative Binomial
Suppose \(X \sim \textnormal{NBin}(r, p).\) Then, \(X\) has PMF \[
P(X = k) = {r + k - 1 \choose k}p^r(1-p)^k
\] with support \(k \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}.\)
If \(X_1, \dots, X_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}\textnormal{Geom}(p),\) then \(X_1 + \dots + X_n \sim \textnormal{NBin}(n, p).\)
Similar to Geometric, if we have trials with independent success probability \(p\) and we keep doing trials until we get \(r\) successes, the number of failures is distributed as \(\textnormal{NBin}(r, p).\)
Using this story, \(\textnormal{NBin}(1, p)\) is the same distribution as \(\textnormal{Geom}(p).\)


A.1.6 Poisson
Suppose \(X \sim \textnormal{Pois}(\lambda).\) Then, \(X\) has PMF \[
P(X = k) = \frac{e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k}{k!}
\] with support \(k \in \{0, 1, 2, \dots\}.\)
If we have \(n\) trials that are weakly dependent or independent, and the probability of success for the \(i\)th trial for \(i \in \{1, \dots, n\}\) is equal to \(p_i\) where the \(p_i\)’s are small and \(n\) is large, the number of successes is approximately distributed as \(\textnormal{Pois}\left(n(p_1+\dots+p_n)\right).\)


A.1.7 Hypergeometric
Suppose \(X \sim \textnormal{HGeom}(w, b, n).\) Then, \(X\) has PMF \[
P(X = k) = \frac{{w \choose k}{b \choose n-k}}{{w+b \choose n}},
\] with \(k \in \{0, \dots, n\}.\)
If we have \(w\) white cubes and \(b\) black cubes and we pick \(n\) of them without replacement, then the number of white cubes we draw is distributed as \(\textnormal{HGeom}(w, b, n).\)

title: Appendix A — Distributions
text:
A.2 Continuous Distributions

A.2.1 Uniform
Suppose \(X \sim \textnormal{Unif}(a, b).\) Then, \(X\) has PDF \[
f(x) = \frac{1}{b-a},
\] where \(x \in (a, b).\)
If we pick a uniform random number between \(a\) and \(b\) with \(a < b,\) then the distribution of that number is \(\textnormal{Unif}(a, b).\)


A.2.2 Beta
Suppose \(X \sim \textnormal{Beta}(\alpha, \beta).\) Then, \(X\) has PDF \[
f(x) = \frac{\Gamma(\alpha + \beta)}{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)}x^{\alpha-1}(1-x)^{\beta-1} \propto x^{\alpha-1}(1-x)^{\beta-1},
\] where \(x \in (0, 1).\)
The \(\textnormal{Beta}(1, 1)\) distribution is equivalent to the \(\textnormal{Unif}(0, 1)\) distribution.


A.2.3 Expo
Suppose \(X \sim \textnormal{Expo}(\lambda).\) Then, \(X\) has PDF \[
f(x) = \lambda e^{-\lambda x},
\] where \(x \in (0, \infty).\)


A.2.4 Weibull
Suppose \(X \sim \Wei(\lambda, \gamma).\) Then, \(X\) has PDF \[
f(x) = \gamma \lambda e^{-\lambda x^\gamma}x^{\gamma - 1},
\] where \(x \in (0, \infty).\)
If \(X \sim \textnormal{Expo}(\lambda),\) then \(X^{1/\gamma} \sim \Wei(\lambda, \gamma).\)


A.2.5 Pareto
Suppose \(X \sim \Pareto(m, \alpha).\) Then, \(X\) has PDF \[
f(x) = \frac{\alpha m^\alpha}{x^{\alpha+1}} \propto \frac{1}{x^{\alpha+1}},
\] with \(x \geq m.\)
If \(Y \sim \textnormal{Expo}(\lambda),\) then \(me^Y \sim \Pareto(m, \lambda).\)
Similarly, if \(Y \sim \Pareto(m, \lambda),\) then \(\log Y - \log m \sim \textnormal{Expo}(\lambda).\)


A.2.6 Gamma
Suppose \(X \sim \textnormal{Gamma}(a, \lambda).\) Then, \(X\) has PDF \[
f(x) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(a)}(\lambda x)^a e^{-\lambda x} \frac{1}{x} \propto x^{a-1}e^{-\lambda x},
\] where \(x \in (0, \infty).\) Here, \(\Gamma\) is the Gamma function, defined as \[
\Gamma(a) = \int_0^\infty z^{a-1}e^{-z}dz.
\]
If \(X_1, \dots, X_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}\textnormal{Expo}(\lambda),\) then \(X_1 + \dots + X_n \sim \textnormal{Gamma}(n, \lambda).\)


A.2.7 Normal
Suppose \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2).\) Then, \(X\) has PDF \[
f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}} \propto \exp\left(-\frac{(x-\mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right),
\] where \(x \in \mathbb{R}.\)


A.2.8 Chi-Square
Suppose \(X \sim \chi^2_n.\) Then, \(X\) has PDF \[
f(x) = \frac{1}{2^{n/2}\Gamma(n/2)}x^{n/2-1}e^{-x/2},
\] where \(x \in (0, \infty).\)
The \(\chi^2_n\) distribution is equivalent to the \(\textnormal{Gamma}(n/2, 1/2)\) distribution.
If \(Z_1, \dots, Z_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}\mathcal{N}(0, 1),\) then \(Z_1^2 + \dots + Z_n^2 \sim \chi^2_n.\)


A.2.9 Student-t
Suppose \(X \sim t_n.\) Then, \(X\) has PDF \[
f(x) = \frac{\Gamma((n+1)/2)}{\Gamma(n/2)\sqrt{n\pi}}\left(1+\frac{x^2}{n}\right)^{-(n+1)/2},
\] where \(x \in \mathbb{R}.\)
If \(Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1)\) and \(V \sim \chi^2_n\) with \(Z\) and \(V\) independent, then \[
\frac{Z}{\sqrt{V/n}} \sim t_n.
\]

title: Appendix A — Distributions
text:
A.3 Multivariate Distributions

A.3.1 Multinomial
Suppose \(X = (X_1, \dots, X_k) \sim \textnormal{Mult}_k(n, p),\) where \(p = (p_1, \dots, p_k).\) Then, \(X\) has joint PMF \[
P(X_1 = x_1, \dots, X_k = x_k) = \frac{n!}{x_1! \dots x_k!} p_1^{x_1} \dots p_k^{x_k}
\] with \(x_i \geq 0\) for all \(i \in \{1, \dots, k\}\) and \(x_1 + \dots + x_k = n.\)
Suppose we have \(n\) independent trials, each of which ends with one of \(k\) different outcomes. If \(p_i\) is the probability that each trial ends up in outcome \(i,\) then the vector \((X_1, \dots, X_k)\) with \(X_i\) being the number of trials that end up in outcome \(i\) is distributed \(\textnormal{Mult}_k(n, p)\) with \(p = (p_1, \dots, p_k).\)


A.3.2 Multivariate Normal (MVN)
Suppose \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma),\) where \(X = (X_1, \dots, X_k) \in \mathbb{R}^k.\) Then, \(X\) has joint PDF \[\begin{align*}
f(x) &= \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{k/2}|\Sigma|^{1/2}}\exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^T\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)\right)\\
&\propto \exp\left(-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu)^T\Sigma^{-1}(x-\mu)\right),
\end{align*}\] where \(x = (x_1, \dots, x_k) \in \mathbb{R}^k.\) Here, \(|\Sigma|\) is the determinant of \(\Sigma.\)
Let \(\Sigma_{ij}\) be the \((ij)\)-element of \(\Sigma.\) Then, it is true that \(\textnormal{Var}(X_i) = \Sigma_{ii}\) and \(\textnormal{Cov}(X_i, X_j) = \Sigma_{ij}.\)
If \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma),\) then for any \(n\)-tuple of real numbers \((a_i, \dots, a_k),\) we have that \(a_1X_1 + \dots + a_kX_k\) is Normal, where we take constants to be Normal with zero variance.
If \(X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \Sigma)\) and \(\textnormal{Cov}(X_i, X_j) = 0,\) then \(X_i\) and \(X_j\) are independent. That is, uncorrelatedness and independence imply each other within an MVN.

title: Appendix B — Useful Results and Definitions
Appendix B — Useful Results and Definitions
text:
B.1.1 Multiplication Rule
Consider a series of two trials, where the first trial has \(a\) possible outcomes and for each of these outcomes, the second trial has \(b\) possible outcomes. Then the total number of possible pairs of outcomes from the two trials is \(ab.\)


B.1.2 Naive Definition of Probability
Let \(S\) be the sample space where every outcome is equally likely. If \(A \subseteq S\) is an event, then
\[
P(A) = \frac{|A|}{|S|},
\]
the number of outcomes in \(A\) divided by the number of outcomes in \(S.\)


B.1.3 Principle of Inclusion Exclusion (PIE)
If \(A\) and \(B\) are events, then
\[
P(A \cup B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A, B).
\]
If \(C\) is also an event, then
\[
P(A \cup B \cup C) = P(A) + P(B) + P(C) - P(A,B)-P(A,C)-P(B,C)+P(A,B,C).
\]
Could be good to put Venn diagrams here.
In general, if \(A_1, \dots, A_n\) are events, then
\[
P\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^n A_i\right) = \sum_{i} P(A_i) - \sum_{i < j}P(A_i,A_j) + \sum_{i < j < k} P(A_i,A_j,A_k) - \dots + (-1)^{n+1}P(A_1, \dots, A_n).
\]
In words, we add all of the single probabilities up, then subtract all the pairwise probabilities, then add back all the triplet probabilities, subtract all the quadruple probabilities, etc.


B.1.4 Binomial Coefficients
If there are \(n\) distinct objects and we pick \(k\) of them without replacement, then the total number of possible sets of \(k\) objects (in other words, order does not matter) we can end up with is given by
\[
{n \choose k} = \frac{n!}{(n-k)!k!}.
\]


B.1.5 Bose-Einstein
If there are \(n\) distinct objects and we choose \(k\) times with replacement and order does not matter, then the total number of ways this can happen is
\[
{n + k-1 \choose k}.
\]
If each choice is equally likely to be any of the \(n\) objects and the choices are independent of one another, then these outcomes are not equally likely. Hence, using the naive definition of probability on Bose-Einstein will fail.

title: Appendix B — Useful Results and Definitions
text:
B.2 Conditional Probability

B.2.1 Conditional Probability
If \(A\) and \(B\) are events, then the probability of \(A\) given \(B,\) notated as \(P(A|B),\) is given by
\[
P(A|B) = \frac{P(A,B)}{P(B)}.
\]
The equation above also gives us this equality:
\[
P(A,B) = P(A|B)P(B) = P(B|A)P(A).
\]
Note that \(P(A|B)\) and \(P(B|A)\) are different things. Falsely equating these two is known as the Prosecutor’s Fallacy.


B.2.2 Bayes’ Rule
If \(A\) and \(B\) are events, then \[
P(A|B) = \frac{P(B|A)P(A)}{P(B)}.
\] Moreover, if \(C\) is an event, then we can get Bayes’ Rule with extra conditioning: \[
P(A|B,C) = \frac{P(B|A,C)P(A|C)}{P(B|C)}.
\] Note that we add conditioning on \(C\), and we condition on \(C\) for every single probability in the equation. An easy way to check if you have applied Bayes’ Rule with extra conditioning properly is to see if the additioning condition you’re adding, in this case \(C\), appears after the conditioning bar in every single probability. If there is any probability with no conditioning bar or if there is any probability where \(C\) appears before the conditioning bar, that is incorrect!


B.2.3 Law of Total Probability (LOTP)
Suppose \(A\) is an event and \(B_1, \dots, B_k\) form a partition of the sample space. Then, \[
P(A) = \sum_{i=1}^k P(A|B_i)P(B_i).
\] One useful partition is \(B, B^c,\) in which case we get \[
P(A) = P(A|B)P(B)+P(A|B^c)P(B^c).
\] We can also add extra conditioning on \(C\) to get LOTP with extra conditioning: \[
P(A|C) = \sum_{i=1}^k P(A|B_i,C)P(B_i|C).
\] Once again, note that \(C\) appears after the conditioning bar in every single probability. If there is any probability with no conditioning bar or there is a probability with \(C\) before the conditioning bar when you are doing LOTP with extra conditioning, that is incorrect!
Combining Bayes’ Rule and LOTP, this equation forms, which is very useful: \[
P(A|B) = \frac{P(B|A)P(A)}{P(B|A)P(A)+P(B|A^c)P(A^c)}.
\]


B.2.4 Independence
Two events \(A\) and \(B\) are independent if \[
P(A,B) = P(A)P(B).
\] This is equivalent to \(P(A|B) = P(A).\) Intuitively, independence means that knowing whether one event happened tells you nothing about the probability of the other event happening.
\(A\) and \(B\) are conditionally independent given \(C\) if \[
P(A,B|C) = P(A|C)P(B|C).
\] This means that given \(C,\) knowing additionally whether one event happened still tells you nothing about the probability of the other event happening.
Independence and conditional independence are different things and do not imply each other. Conditional independence given \(C\) also does not imply conditional independence given \(C^c.\) Can you think of examples?

title: Appendix B — Useful Results and Definitions
section: B.3 Random Variables and their Distributions
text:
B.3 Random Variables and their Distributions

B.3.1 Random Variable
Given an experiment with sample space \(S,\) a random variable is a function from \(S\) to the real numbers. For example, if we are rolling a 6-sided die and \(X\) is defined to be two times the side that is rolled, then \(X=2\) when a 1 is rolled, \(X=4\) when a 2 is rolled, etc. Random variables are often shortened to r.v.s. The support of a random variable is the set of possible values that the r.v. can take on. In our example, the support is \(\{2,4,6,8,10,12\}.\)


B.3.2 Probability Mass Functions (PMF)
A Probability Mass Funcction, or PMF, gives the probability of an r.v. taking on each value in its support. That is, for each \(x\) in the support of an r.v. \(X,\) the PMF of \(X\) defines \(P(X=x).\) Every valid PMF should sum up to 1 over its support. A PMF determines a random variable’s distribution.


B.3.3 Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)
A Cumulative Distribution Function, or CDF, of a r.v. \(X\) is a function \(F_X(x)\) that maps the real numbers to \([0,1].\) It is defined as \(F_X(x) = P(X \leq x).\) A CDF must be nondecreasing, right-continuous, and have \(\lim_{x \to -\infty}F(x) = 0\) and \(\lim_{x \to \infty} F(x) = 1.\) A CDF determines a random variable’s distribution.


B.3.4 Distributions
Notable distributions include the Bernoulli, Binomial, Geometric, Negative Binomial, and Hypergeometric, whose definitions can be found in Appendix A.


B.3.5 Indicator Random Variables
Given an event \(A,\) the random variable \(I(A)\) which is equal to 1 when \(A\) occurs and equal to 0 when \(A\) does not occur is known as an indicator random variable. We have that \(I(A) \sim \textnormal{Bern}(P(A))\) since \(I(A)\) is equal to 1 with probability \(P(A)\) and equal to 0 with probability \(1-P(A).\)

title: 4  Expectation?
text:
(Multiple Games) Monty has \(c \geq 3\) cars and \(g \geq 3\) goats, with \(c+g=3n\) for some positive integer \(n\). Monty will play \(n\) games. He first randomly gives each prize a unique label from \(1\) to \(3n,\) with all arrangements equally likely. For the \(i\)th Monty Hall game, he will hide the prize with label \(3i-2\) behind door 1, the prize with label \(3i-1\) behind door 2, and the prize with label \(3i\) behind door 3. So for game 1, prize 1, 2, and 3 will be hidden behind doors 1, 2, and 3, respectively.
Each Monty Hall game will proceed as follows. The contestant will initially pick a door. Then, if the two remaining doors contains a car and a goat, Monty will reveal the goat. If the two doors contain the same prize, then Monty will open one of them with equal probabilities. Then, the contestant will be given the choice to switch.

Consider a single game. What is the distribution of the number of cars behind the three doors?
The contestant employs the following strategy. They will first choose door 1. Then, once Monty reveals a door, they will switch. What is the probability they will win the car?
In a game, the contestant chooses door 1, and Monty reveals a goat behind door 2. What is the probability the car is behind door 3?
Suppose all of the contestants in the \(n\) games decide to use the strategy where they will always switch. What is the expected number of cars that will be won by these contestants in total?







CautionSolution






Let \(C\) be the number of cars behind the three doors. By the Hypergeometric Story, we have \(C \sim \textnormal{HGeom}(c, g, 3).\)
Note that if there are two cars or three cars, the contestant will always win the car with this strategy (why?). If there are zero cars, then the contestant will clearly lose. If there is one car, then we know the contestant has a \(2/3\) probability of winning the car. Hence, letting \(W\) be the event that the contestant wins the car, we have \[\begin{align*}
P(W) &= P(W|C=1)P(C=1)+P(W|C=2)P(C=2)+P(W|C=3)P(C=3)\\
&= \frac{{c \choose 1}{g \choose 2}}{{c+g \choose 3}} \cdot \frac{2}{3} + \frac{{c \choose 2}{g \choose 1}}{{c+g \choose 3}} + \frac{{c \choose 3}}{{c+g \choose 3}}\\
&= \frac{2c{g\choose 2}+3{c\choose2}g+3{c\choose3}}{3{c+g\choose3}}.
\end{align*}\]
Let \(C_i\) be the event that there is a car behind door \(i,\) and \(M_i\) be the event that Monty reveals a goat behind door \(i.\) We have by Bayes’ Rule and LOTP that \[
P(C_3|M_2) = \frac{P(M_2|C_3)P(C_3)}{P(M_2|C_3)P(C_3)+P(M_2|C_3^c)P(C_3^c)}
\] Consider \(P(M_2|C_3).\) If there is a car behind door 3, then all that is required is for there to be a goat behind door 2 for Monty to reveal a goat behind it. Thus, since one car was used for the 3rd door, this probability is \(\frac{g}{c+g-1}.\)

Now consider \(P(M_2|C_3^c).\) If there is a goat behind door 3, then for Monty to reveal a goat behind door 2, we need there to be a goat behind door 2 and for Monty to choose it with \(1/2\) probability. Since one goat was used for the 3rd door, this probability is \(\frac{g-1}{2(c+g-1)}\). Therefore, \[
P(C_3|M_2) = \frac{\frac{g}{c+g-1} \cdot \frac{c}{c+g}}{\frac{g}{c+g-1} \cdot \frac{c}{c+g} + \frac{g-1}{2(c+g-1)} \cdot \frac{g}{c+g}} = \frac{2c}{2c +g-1}.
\]

Let \(I_i\) be the indicator r.v. for the \(i\)th contestant winning a car. We know the probability that \(I_i = 1\) from part (b). Hence, by linearity of expectation and the Fundamental Bridge, we have that \[
E(I_1 + \dots + I_n) = \frac{2c{g\choose 2}+3{c\choose2}g+3{c\choose3}}{3{c+g\choose3}}n.
\]




(Increasing Doors) Monty will host \(N \sim \textnormal{Geom}(p)\) Monty Hall games. The \(i\)th game will have \(i\) doors, with one of them being chosen with equal probabilities to hold a car and the other doors holding goats.
In games 1 and 2, the contestant will pick a door, and they will get whatever prize is behind that door. In game \(g\) with \(g \geq 3,\) the contestant will first pick a door, then Monty, who knows where the goat is, will open \(g-2\) doors that hold goats. If the contestant had initially picked the door with the car behind it, then Monty will randomly open \(g-2\) of the \(g-1\) doors with goats, with all openings equally likely.

Let \(D\) be the total number of doors that appear throughout the \(N\) games. As an example, if \(N = 2,\) then one door appears for the first game and two doors appear for the second game, so \(D\) would be \(3.\) What is \(E(D)\)?
The contestant for each of the games will first pick a door, then switch. What is the probability that throughout the \(N\) games, at least two cars will be won?
After solving the previous part, give a short proof for why \(xe^{1-x}\) is between 0 and 1 for all \(x \in (0, 1).\)







CautionSolution






We know that \(D = 1+\dots+N = \frac{N(N+1)}{2}\) by the sums of integers identity. Therefore, \[
E(D) = \frac{1}{2}(E(N^2)+E(N)).
\] As \(N \sim \textnormal{Geom}(p),\) know that \(E(N) = \frac{1-p}{p}\) and \(E(N^2) = \textnormal{Var}(N)+E(N)^2 = \frac{1-p}{p^2}+\frac{(1-p)^2}{p^2} = \frac{2-3p+p^2}{p^2}.\) Therefore, \[
E(D) = \frac{2-3p+p^2}{2p^2} + \frac{1-p}{2p} = \frac{1-p}{p^2}.
\]
Using the same logic as in Monty Hall, the probability that if game \(i\) is played, the probability that the contestant wins the car is \(\frac{i-1}{i}.\) Hence, the probability that the contestant loses the car is \(\frac{1}{i}.\)
We will condition on the number of games. Let \(C\) be the event that at least two cars are won. We will find \(P(C^c),\) then find \(P(C)\) by using \(P(C) = 1 - P(C^c).\) By LOTP, \[
P(C^c) = \sum_{n=0}^\infty P(C^c|N=n)P(N=n).
\] We know that we need at least \(2\) games for there to be a chance at least two cars are won. Suppose there are \(n \geq 2\) games. We know Game 1 would have resulted in the contestant winning the car (of course). So the only way the contestants do not win at least \(2\) cars is if games 2 and onward all resulted in losses. Since the probability of losing game \(i\) is \(1/i\) for \(i \geq 2,\) we know that this is \[
P(C^c|N=n) = \prod_{i=2}^n \frac{1}{i} = \prod_{i=1}^n \frac{1}{i} = \frac{1}{n!}.
\] Note that when \(n = 0\) or \(n = 1,\) in which the probability less than two cars are won is \(1,\) we indeed get \(1/n! = 1,\) so the equation above is true for all nonnegative integers \(n.\) Now, \[
P(C^c) = \sum_{i=0}^\infty \frac{1}{i!}P(N=i) = p\sum_{i=0}^\infty \frac{(1-p)^i}{i!} = pe^{1-p}.
\] We could’ve also found the sum by pattern-matching with the Poisson PDF: \[
P(C^c) = p\sum_{i=0}^\infty \frac{(1-p)^i}{i!} = pe^{1-p}\sum_{i=0}^\infty \frac{e^{-(1-p)}(1-p)^i}{i!} = pe^{1-p},
\] where the sum becomes the sum of the \(\textnormal{Pois}(1-p)\) PMF, which must be 1. Therefore, the probability that at least two cars will be won is \[
P(C) = 1 - pe^{1-p}.
\] \[
P(C^c) = pe^{1-p}
\]
We see that \(P(C^c) = pe^{1-p},\) which is a probability and hence must be between 0 and 1. This shows that \(xe^{1-x}\) is between 0 and 1 for all \(x \in (0, 1).\)




(Maximum Monty) Monty Hall has \(n\) doors. Exactly \(c\) cars will be hidden, where \(c\) is an integer between 1 and \(n,\) inclusive, with each integer being equally likely to be picked. After \(c\) is chosen, the cars will be randomly hidden behind \(c\) different doors, with each door equally likely to be picked, and the remaining doors will hold goats. A contestant on this show will, on each turn, either ask Monty to open a door or ask to end the game. If the contestant asks Monty to open a door, Monty will open a door with a goat if one remains; otherwise, Monty will use his wizard powers to turn one random car into a goat (without the contestant noticing) and open the door containing the new goat. The contestant cannot tell the difference between a goat and a goat that used to be a car. Afterwards, the contestant must choose between asking Monty to open another door and ending the game.
If the contestant asks for the game to end, Monty will reveal the prizes behind all of the remaining doors. If even \(one\) of the unopened doors contains a goat, the contestant walks away with nothing. Otherwise, the contestant will win all of the cars that remain. A contestant wants to maximize the expected number of cars they will win. What strategy should they use, and what is the expected number of cars they will win by using it?






CautionSolution





Let \(G\) be the number of goats originally in the game. Let \(X_k\) be the number of cars the contestant will win if they ask for the game to end after \(k\) turns. We have \(X_k = 0\) if \(k < G\) and \(X_k = n - k\) if \(k \geq G.\) Therefore, we have \[
E(X_k) = (n-k)P(G \leq k) = \frac{(n-k)(k+1)}{n},
\] where we assume \(k < n\) (as there is at least one car, there is no reason the contestant should ask Monty to open another door when all but one door have been opened). We can rewrite this as \[
E(X_k) = -\frac{1}{n}k^2+\frac{n-1}{n}k+1,
\] which is a downward sloping parabola, meaning it achieves its maximum value at its vertex. The coordinate of the vertex is \(-((n-1)/n)/(-2/n) = (n-1)/2.\) Therefore, if \(n\) is odd, we should set \(k = (n-1)/2\) to maximize the expected number of cars won. Otherwise, by symmetry of the parabola, we can either have \(k = n/2\) or \((n-2)/2\) to maximize the expected value.
If \(n\) is odd, we have \[
E(X_{(n-1)/2}) = \frac{(n+1)^2}{4n}.
\] If \(n\) is even, we have \[
E(X_{n/2}) = \frac{n+2}{4} = E(X_{(n-2)/2}).
\]
